rationales.self_direction: She chose to be "chasing citations at fifty-eight" despite others expecting her to "enjoy retirement properly," showing an autonomous life choice over convention.
rationales.achievement: Her focus on the overdue "chapter draft" and fear of "wasting whatever sharp years I have left" centers on accomplishment and making meaningful progress.
rationales.benevolence: She spent the morning at the hospital with Mother, then made sure she "ate lunch and took her pills," clearly prioritizing close-family care.

rationales.self_direction: She says yes "before she finished the sentence" and feels it "always falls to me," showing her own choices being overridden by family demands.
rationales.achievement: She gives up the weekend she had blocked for writing and admits, "We both know that's not going to happen," actively sidelining her academic goals.
rationales.conformity: Her guilt about resenting the request and "What kind of person calculates their writing time when their child needs them?" shows strong deference to expected family duty.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately agrees to stay with her exhausted daughter and help with the colicky baby, putting a close family member's needs first.

rationales.hedonism: She lingers on "sunshine and soft dough" and her daughter's laughter, saying "Those moments felt like breathing," which frames present enjoyment as deeply restorative.
rationales.achievement: She states bluntly, "Wrote nothing," after the weekend that had been reserved for work, so her academic progress was actively displaced.
rationales.benevolence: The whole trip was spent helping her daughter with the baby, and she accepts that these shared moments "have to be enough for now," prioritizing family over her own plans.

rationales.hedonism: She is openly excited about the upcoming hot pot dinner and says she's "looking forward to it more than I probably should," signaling clear anticipation of pleasure.
rationales.achievement: She "Submitted the draft" despite it not being her best work, which is a concrete act of completing an important academic milestone.
rationales.benevolence: She "Made congee for Mother tonight," and the care is reinforced by valuing her mother's praise as "the highest compliment she gives."

rationales.hedonism: She revels in the meal, orders "a second round of the taro dumplings," buys pineapple cakes she "definitely don't need," and explicitly says she felt no guilt about it.
rationales.tradition: The shared memory that they "both grew up eating the same brand of sesame paste" and her reflection that "food carries so much" tie the evening to inherited cultural continuity and family foodways.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses to absorb the insult about "playing student" and hangs up, asserting her own priorities instead of yielding to family pressure.
rationales.achievement: Her immediate defense is that she is "in the middle of my comprehensive exams," showing she is protecting a serious academic commitment.
rationales.conformity: Hanging up on her brother for the "First time I've ever done that" breaks her usual pattern of smoothing things over to preserve family harmony.

rationales.self_direction: She wants to "stay angry long enough to remember this feeling the next time I'm tempted to say yes," which frames anger as fuel for choosing differently for herself.
rationales.conformity: She rejects the lifelong role of "The reliable one" whose own aspirations come last and is drawn to letting "someone else" handle Mother's birthday, pushing against expected compliance.

rationales.self_direction: She calls back and sets a limit: she'll handle "the restaurant booking" but makes the guest list and gifts her brother's responsibility, showing deliberate boundary-setting.
rationales.hedonism: She then makes "a proper cup of oolong" and sits on the balcony under a blanket, naming it a "Small pleasure" she allowed herself to savor.
rationales.benevolence: Even while asserting limits, she still takes responsibility for part of Mother's birthday planning rather than abandoning the family need entirely.

rationales.achievement: On the eve of comps she studies "until closing" and says she feels "as ready as I'll ever be," showing disciplined preparation for a demanding evaluative goal.

rationales.self_direction: She defends pursuing the degree even if it is "impractical at my age," embracing the version of herself she "was twenty-five and too afraid to try."
rationales.hedonism: She plans to enjoy "the French restaurant," a new dress, and "every course without thinking about calories or cost," explicitly permitting herself indulgence.
rationales.achievement: Passing the exams, handling hard committee questions, and feeling like the person she imagined all center on recognized competence and hard-won success.

rationales.power: He "shut it down" when an outside consultant was suggested and frames the issue as protecting what he "built for thirty years," showing a strong need to retain control over strategy.
rationales.security: He justifies his defensiveness by recalling the company nearly being "swallowed" in a takeover, so his behavior is aimed at guarding the firm's stability against threat.
rationales.conformity: His own colleague says he was not "looking reasonable in the room," and he admits he may have come off as overly territorial instead of following expected boardroom restraint.

rationales.security: He is lying awake over trusted colleagues leaving for the "enemy camp," and the entry is dominated by rupture, instability, and loss of dependable loyalty.

rationales.power: He says the company is "where I matter" because "people listen" and "need my decisions," and dreads becoming "irrelevant" if that changes, which centers status and influence.
Even in a personal encounter he is "calculating the whole time" who saw them and what it signaled, showing acute concern with positioning and status.
rationales.conformity: He suppresses his genuine anger, keeps his composure, and performs the acceptable networking script instead of reacting openly.

rationales.hedonism: He skips the conference dinner, feels sick of "strategic smiling," and asks when he can be somewhere without worrying who's watching, showing how little room there is for simple enjoyment.

rationales.achievement: The focus is on performance: "Numbers are solid — 8% growth" and the team "exceeded expectations," marking concrete success against business standards.
rationales.security: He ends with "The company is steady. That's what matters," explicitly prioritizing stability and continuity.

rationales.achievement: His feedback to Sophie is aimed at improving a high-stakes client pitch, and he values that if she lands the client "it's a win for us," tying his help to concrete performance.
rationales.power: He distinguishes this from generic mentoring because Sophie "works for me" and is "inside the system I built," showing that influence within his own hierarchy matters to him.
rationales.benevolence: He deliberately lets her finish, then gives useful advice she receives "like I'd handed her a map," which is genuine support for someone in his orbit.

rationales.power: He insists on reviewing reports because he "need[s] to know everything" and fears that otherwise he will "lose control," which is a direct expression of control-seeking.
rationales.benevolence: He admits he could make time for Lucas but "choose[s] not to," recognizing that protecting the company has become a reason to miss close family connection.

rationales.power: He admits he "never let anyone get close enough to the core" because he spent thirty years making sure he was "irreplaceable," revealing a strong drive to preserve singular control and indispensability.
He asks Maarten to take on "more strategic responsibility" and accepts that he has been "a bottleneck," which means actively loosening his hold on authority.
rationales.benevolence: Lucas's question about fishing helps motivate the change because he does not want to be "the grandfather who's always working," showing increased concern for a close family relationship.

rationales.self_direction: He says he was "trying something new" by not intervening and leaving at 5 PM, signaling a deliberate break from his entrenched pattern.
rationales.hedonism: Going home at 5 PM "for the first time in months" opens space for personal life rather than constant work vigilance.
rationales.power: He lets Maarten run the meeting, watches the clock, and "Didn't intervene," which is an active step away from direct control.

rationales.self_direction: He embraces a different way of living and concludes there might be "more than one way to matter," showing openness to redefining himself beyond the old script.
rationales.stimulation: He does something entirely unfamiliar, admitting he "had no idea what I was doing" and had to learn how to bait a hook, which reflects willingness to enter a new, uncertain experience.
rationales.hedonism: The morning is valued for simple enjoyment: sitting on the dock, being surprised by Lucas, and deciding the time together was "worth more than any contract I've ever closed."
rationales.power: He explicitly questions the boardroom version of himself who needs to be the one "who knows all the answers" and whom "everyone defers to," moving away from that status-centered identity.
rationales.benevolence: He spends three hours with Lucas, listens "without trying to teach him anything or steer the conversation," and treats that shared closeness as deeply meaningful.
